key_insight: A Monte Carlo framework can turn published aggregate trial results into patient-level risk predictions for rare cancers, demonstrated in Ewing sarcoma with multi-timepoint biomarker and treatment modeling.
clinical_relevance: This could help refine survivorship surveillance and future trial stratification, but it is not yet a substitute for patient-specific validated clinical decision support.
method: Six-stage discrete-event Monte Carlo simulation built from published Ewing sarcoma trial data.
Integrates genetic risk, serial biomarker dynamics, post-surgical ctDNA-MRD, and treatment-related mortality as a competing risk.
Adds organ-specific long-term toxicity projections from chemotherapy and radiation exposure.
evidence_signal: Validated against data from more than 3,400 patients, the model reported 3.2% mean absolute error across 23 efficacy endpoints and stayed within published confidence intervals for all 20 toxicity endpoints.
watch_item: The framework is validated on aggregate trial data, so transfer to other rare cancers and real-world patient-level use still needs prospective validation.
paper_title: Multi-Timepoint Risk Stratification in Rare Cancers: A Computational Framework Validated against Published Ewing Sarcoma Trial Data
